coredata.dc:title: A click-induced fluorescence-quenching sensor based on gold nanoparticles for detection of copper(Ⅱ) ion and ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Shijun
Liu, Shuwen
Dai, Guofei
Zhang, Xiaojin
Xia, Fan
Dai, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), as one of the major tools of fluorescence-quenching sensors, have been widely used in biochemical analysis through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect. With the development of chemistry and biochemistry, reaction-based fluorescent sensors have attracted increasing attention because of their unique selectivity, high sensitivity and rapid detection. Here we report a click-induced fluorescence-quenching sensor based on AuNPs for detection of copper ion (Cu2+) and ascorbic acid (AA). A fluorescent dye Rhodamine B-(CH2CH2O)4-azide (RB-PEG4-N3) is conjugated to the surface of AuNPs via Cu(I)-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition (CuAAC) in the presence of Cu2+ and AA, leading to the fluorescence quenching of Rhodamine B. The sensor responds quickly (within less than 3 min). The fluorescent intensity linearly correlates with the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0–108 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ is 5.8 μM. The concentration range for detection of AA is 0–3 mM. The LOD for AA is 0.13 mM. In addition, the application of the sensor is verified by performing detection in real samples such as fresh orange, juice, VC tablet, and lake water. This work expands the application of AuNPs-based fluorescence-quenching sensors for multi-component detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Fluorescence-quenching
Click chemistry
Copper(Ⅱ) ion
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in real samples
Detection of AA in real samples
Synthesis and characterization of alkyne-modified AuNPs
Detection of Cu2+ and AA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption spectrum (a), SEM image (b), TEM image (c) of alkyne-modified AuNPs. The corresponding histogram of particle size distribution from the TEM image for AuNPs with Gaussian fit (d).

Fluorescence emission spectra of alkyne-modified AuNPs, RB-PEG4-N3, and AA with the addition of Cu2+ (a). The final concentrations of Cu2+ are 0, 18, 36, 54, 72, 90, and 108 μM from top to bottom and the excitation wavelength is 570 nm. The linear relationship of the fluorescence intensity at 591 nm versus the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0–108 μM (b). Fluorescence change of the mixture of alkyne-modified AuNPs, RB-PEG4-N3, and AA with the addition of different metal ions (c). n = 3 per group. Data are mean ± s.d.

Fluorescence emission spectra of alkyne-modified AuNPs, RB-PEG4-N3, and Cu2+ with the addition of AA (a). The final concentrations of AA are 0, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3 mM from top to bottom and the excitation wavelength is 570 nm. The linear relationship of the fluorescence intensity at 591 nm versus the concentration of AA in the range of 0–3 mM (b). Fluorescence change of the mixture of alkyne-modified AuNPs, RB-PEG4-N3, and Cu2+ with the addition of different organics (c). n = 3 per group. Data are mean ± s.d.
The concentration of Cu2+ in three lakes detected through our sensor and ICP-MS. n = 3 per group. Data are mean ± s.d.

The concentration of AA in fresh orange, juice, and VC tablet detected through our sensor, standard quantity, and VC assay kit. n = 3 per group. Data are mean ± s.d. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of a click-induced fluorescence-quenching sensor.
Comparison of the methods for simultaneous detection of Cu2+ and AA.

Opening of the nanopores after sequential addition of AA

High stability, selectivity, a near real-time response, and an extremely small dead-volume
Fluorescence quenching by the formation of the Cu2+-CQDs complexes
Fluorescence recovery by the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu + after the addition of AA
High sensitivity, high selectivity, low-cost, simple naked-eye readout, and good linearity

Fluorescence recovery by AA taking away metal ions from the complexes
High sensitivity, good selectivity, and low-cost
Fluorescence quenching due to electron or energy transfer between Cu2+ and BSA-AuNCs
Fluorescence recovery due to AA competitive binding with Cu2+
High sensitivity, high selectivity, water-dispersion, high stability, and good biocompatibility
Fluorescence quenching induced by Cu2+-AA-catalyzed click reaction
Fluorescence quenching induced by Cu2+-AA-catalyzed click reaction

head.ce:title: A click-induced fluorescence-quenching sensor based on gold nanoparticles for detection of copper(Ⅱ) ion and ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan, 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan 430074, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Water Resources and Environment of Poyang Lake, Jiangxi Institute of Water Sciences, Nanchang, 330029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Water Resources and Environment of Poyang Lake

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Water Resources and Environment of Poyang Lake, Jiangxi Institute of Water Sciences, Nanchang 330029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), as one of the major tools of fluorescence-quenching sensors, have been widely used in biochemical analysis through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect. With the development of chemistry and biochemistry, reaction-based fluorescent sensors have attracted increasing attention because of their unique selectivity, high sensitivity and rapid detection. Here we report a click-induced fluorescence-quenching sensor based on AuNPs for detection of copper ion (Cu2+) and ascorbic acid (AA). A fluorescent dye Rhodamine B-(CH2CH2O)4-azide (RB-PEG4-N3) is conjugated to the surface of AuNPs via Cu(I)-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition (CuAAC) in the presence of Cu2+ and AA, leading to the fluorescence quenching of Rhodamine B. The sensor responds quickly (within less than 3 min). The fluorescent intensity linearly correlates with the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0–108 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ is 5.8 μM. The concentration range for detection of AA is 0–3 mM. The LOD for AA is 0.13 mM. In addition, the application of the sensor is verified by performing detection in real samples such as fresh orange, juice, VC tablet, and lake water. This work expands the application of AuNPs-based fluorescence-quenching sensors for multi-component detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have attracted extensive attention in recent decades due to their nice nanostructures and unique optical properties [1]. In particular, the high extinction coefficient of AuNPs makes they can be used as an energy acceptor for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) in sensing [2]. For example, Yi et al. designed a fluorescent sensor composed of AuNPs and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to detect glutathione (GSH) [3]. Adding CQDs to the colloidal solution of AuNPs induces the aggregation of AuNPs and CQDs, resulting in the color change of AuNPs and the fluorescence quenching of CQDs. GSH can prevent AuNPs from agglomerating and expand the distance between particles, thereby producing color changes and fluorescence signal recovery. Such fluorescent sensors depend on the FRET effect through the aggregation of AuNPs and other substances [4–8].

The fluorescence quenching due to FRET requires the distance between the donor and acceptor to be sufficiently close (<10 nm) [9], and the chemical reaction on the surface of AuNPs can become the bridge of the FRET effect [10]. For example, Ren et al. developed a fluorescent sensor to detect hydrogen peroxide through the surface chemical reaction of AuNPs [11]. In the presence of H2O2, the conjugation between tyramide-labeled tetramethylrhodamine and protein-conjugated AuNPs triggered a tyramine reaction, leading to a change in fluorescence signal. In addition to the tyramide reaction, click reactions because of high efficiency, mild reaction conditions and high functional group selectivity are often applied in chemical sensors [12].

Cu(I)-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition (CuAAC) is one of the most popular click reactions [13]. Cu2+ can be reduced to monovalent Cu+ with the action of ascorbic acid (AA) [14]. The concentrations of Cu2+ and AA can regulate the occurrence of CuAAC [15]. For example, Jiang et al. reported a copper detection system based on CuAAC and FRET between the alkyne-functionalized graphene oxide (GO) and carboxyrhodamine110-azide (Rho-N3) [16]. With the addition of AA, Cu2+ is converted to Cu+, which induces the linkage of Rho-N3 to the surface of GO. GO as an energy acceptor efficiently quenches the fluorescence of Rho-N3. It is worth mentioning the role of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as an energy acceptor in the development for click-induced detection of Cu2+ and AA [17–20].

In this work, we develop a click-induced fluorescence-quenching sensor based on AuNPs to detect Cu2+ and AA (Scheme 1 ). The alkyne group is modified on the surface of AuNPs through ligand exchange. Rhodamine B-(CH2CH2O)4-azide (RB-PEG4-N3) is conjugated to the surface of AuNPs via CuAAC in the presence of Cu2+ and AA. Through CuAAC, the distance between AuNPs and Rhodamine B is 3.8 nm [(0.154 × 16 + 0.148 × 2 + 0.143 × 12 + 0.182) nm × sin54° = 3.8 nm], which meets the requirement of the FRET effect [10]. AuNPs are an energy acceptor and will lead to the fluorescence quenching of Rhodamine B. The sensor can directly detect the concentration of the analytes in the aqueous solution, without adding other substances. We further apply the sensor to detect Cu2+ in lake water and AA in fresh oranges, fruit juice, and VC tablet.

Hydrochloric acid (36.0%–38.0%), nitric acid (65.0%–68.0%), trisodium citrate solution, ascorbic acid (AA), and sodium hydroxide (96.0%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Thiol-(CH2CH2O)6-alkyne (SH-PEG6-CCH), Thiol-(CH2)11-(OCH2CH2)6-hydroxyl (SH-(CH2)11-PEG3-OH), and Rhodamine B-(CH2CH2O)4-azide (RB-PEG4-N3) were purchased from Hunan Huateng Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd. (Hunan China). All metal salts such as AlCl3, BaCl2, Mn(NO3)2, KCl, ZnCl2, CaCl2, NaCl, FeCl2, FeCl3, MgCl2, CuSO4, and HAuCl4·4H2O were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Analytes (dopamine, glucose, oxalic acid, glycine, glutathione, fructose, mannose, and histidine) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Vitamin C (VC) assay kit was purchased from Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute (Nanjing, China). VC tablet was purchased from local pharmacy. Orange juice and fresh orange were purchased from local supermarket. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment. Other chemicals were of analytical grade and directly used without any further purification.

A stirred aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (20 mM, 5 mL) was heated to reflux and trisodium citrate solution (38.3 mM, 10 mL) was added quickly. The color of solution changed from yellow to deep red. After the color change, the solution was heated under reflux for an additional 40 min and allowed to cool to room temperature. The product was washed with deionized water three times (8000 rpm, 8 min) and dispersed with deionized water (7.5 mL).

The above AuNPs solution (6 mL) was diluted by adding deionized water (60 mL). Then, the solution was adjusted to pH 9 by NaOH. After that, SH-PEG6-CCH (100 mM, 2.4 mL) and SH-(CH2)11-PEG3-OH (100 mM, 1.2 mL) were added. The mixture was stirred for 24 h and then centrifuged (13000 rpm, 20 min).

For detection of Cu2+, alkyne-modified AuNPs was mixed with RB-PEG4-N3 (10 mM, 1 μL) and a desired amount of AA solution (100 mM, 3 μL) was added. The total volume of the mixture was 100 μL. The final concentration of AA was 3 mM. Then, the different amounts of Cu2+ solution were added. The final concentrations of Cu2+ ranged from 0 to 108 μM. After mildly stirring the mixture in darkness for 3 min, the fluorescence measurements were performed.

For detection of AA, the different amounts of AA solution were added to the mixture of alkyne-modified AuNPs and RB-PEG4-N3. The total volume of the mixture was 100 μL. The final concentration of AA ranged from 0 to 3 mM. Then, a desired amount of Cu2+ solution was added. The final concentration of Cu2+ was 108 μM. After mildly stirring the mixture in darkness for 3 min, the fluorescence measurements were performed.

To investigate the selectivity of the sensor, other metal ions (such as Al3+, Ba2+, Mn2+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Mg2+) and AA were added to the mixture of alkyne-modified AuNPs and RB-PEG4-N3. The final concentration of metal ion was 108 μM. Other organics (such as dopamine, glucose, oxalic acid, glycine, glutathione, fructose, mannose, and histidine) and Cu2+ were added to the mixture of alkyne-modified AuNPs and RB-PEG4-N3. The final concentration of organics was 3 mM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The water from different lakes was gathered in order to detect Cu2+. The lake water was followed to rest for 24 h and large particle sediment was removed by filtration. Then, activated carbon was added to the samples in order to adsorb colored substances. The lake water was filtered through a 0.45 μM PES membrane, put into bottles for use [21]. 50 μM of Cu2+ was added to the lake water as a standard for further testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three kinds of real samples such as fresh orange, orange juice, and VC tablet were chosen to detect AA. The fresh orange needs to be pretreated in advance. First, oxalic acid (0.2 mg mL−1, 1.50 mL) was mixed with orange [22]. The mixture was treated by mashing and centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μM PES membrane. For orange juice, an appropriate amount of juice was added to deionized water and the mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min. 1/4 of VC tablet was crushed and deionized water was added. Then, the mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min. For comparison, these three samples were also used to verify analysis accuracy by VC assay kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of alkyne-modified AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Alkyne-modified AuNPs were synthesized through reduction of HAuCl4 in the presence of trisodium citrate, followed by ligand exchange using SH-PEG6-CCH. Alkyne-modified AuNPs have good dispersion and stability in deionized water, which can be stored at 4 °C for months. Alkyne-modified AuNPs have a single UV–Vis absorption band at 531 nm (Fig. 1 a) and a uniform size distribution from SEM (Fig. 1b) and TEM (Fig. 1c) images. The mean diameter of alkyne-modified AuNPs is estimated as 28.4 nm from analysis of 680 individual particles in TEM images (Fig. 1d). The mean diameter of unmodified AuNPs and alkyne-modified AuNPs in aqueous solution are 21.0 nm and 24.4 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). After ligand exchange, zeta potential of AuNPs increases from −34.1 mV to −19.2 mV (Fig. S2). These results show that alkyne-modified AuNPs have been successfully prepared. In addition, the preparation process of alkyne-modified AuNPs is facile and environmentally friendly.

The sensitivity of the sensor for detection Cu2+ was investigated. To explore detectable concentration of Cu2+, different amounts of Cu2+ were added into the mixture of alkyne-modified AuNPs, RB-PEG4-N3, and AA. The fluorescence intensity at 591 nm gradually decreases with the increase of Cu2+ (Fig. 2 a). There is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0–108 μM (Fig. 2b). The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ is 5.8 μM, although the LOD for Cu2+ of some methods can reach nanomolar or picomolar level [23–25]. LOD = 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 11) and S represents the calculated slope of the linear fitted curve. In this part, excess AA is added to allow the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+. On the basis of Cu(I)-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition (CuAAC), Cu2+ in the presence of AA will induce the fluorescence quenching (Fig. S3). With the increase of excitation wavelength from 500 to 570 nm, the fluorescence intensity shows variant behavior. The fluorescence emission with the excitation wavelength of 570 nm is relatively strongest, which overlaps with the absorption peak of AuNPs (Fig. S4). Energy transfer occurs through CuAAC, which is the cause of the fluorescence quenching during detection [26].

To evaluate the selectivity of the sensor, other metal ions such as Al3+, Ba2+, Mn2+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Mg2+ were examined using the sensor under optimized conditions. These metal ions can't cause the significant decrease of fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2c). The fluorescence quenching of alkyne-modified AuNPs and RB-PEG4-N3 in the presence of Cu2+ and AA is rapid and the quenching completion time is approximately 3 min (Fig. S5). The presence of Cu2+ generates a significantly decreased fluorescence signal, indicating that the sensor is capable of detecting trace amount of Cu2+. The high selectivity of the sensor originates from the high specificity of CuAAC.

The fluorescence emission spectra of alkyne-modified AuNPs, RB-PEG4-N3, and Cu2+ after adding AA were recorded. The final concentration of Cu2+ is 108 μM. The different amounts of AA were added into the mixture. The final concentrations of AA are 0, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3 mM. The fluorescence intensity at 591 nm of the mixtures decreases gradually depending on the increasing concentration of AA (Fig. 3 a). There is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of AA (Fig. 3b). The LOD for AA is 0.13 mM, although the LOD for AA of some methods can reach micromolar or nanomolar level [27–29]. The selectivity of the sensor for detection of AA was evaluated by testing the response to some organics including dopamine, glucose, oxalic acid, glycine, glutathione, fructose, mannose, and histidine. In the end, these organics cannot cause the decrease of the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3c). It is clear that only AA can induce click chemistry, demonstrating its high selectivity for AA. Furthermore, the organics such as glucose and mannose may show interference for detection [30]. The results indicate that our sensor is able to detect millimolar level of AA and resist interference from most organisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To test the practicability of our sensor, actual samples were selected to verify the probe's ability to detect Cu2+ or AA. The detection of Cu2+ in three lakes using our sensor was carried out with ICP-MS as a standard (Fig. 4 ). Our sensor to determine the concentration of Cu2+ in actual water samples is in good agreement with ICP-MS, which shows that our sensor has great potential in monitoring Cu2+. In addition, our sensor is relatively simple and convenient, avoiding complex synthesis steps.

To test the potential application in practical sample analysis, AA in orange, juice, and VC tablet was analyzed through our sensor. We compared the test results with the VC assay kit and standard quantity. The standard quantity of AA in juice and VC tablet was calculated through drug insert and juice ingredient list. The data of our sensor are closer to the standard quantity than the data detected using VC assay kit (Fig. 5 ). The result indicates that our sensor is cheap and reliable in detecting AA, could be expanded for the analysis of real samples.

In general, the sensor shows good analytical performance in terms of sensitivity and selectivity, although the LOD for Cu2+ and AA is higher than other methods (Table 1 ). However, the simple fabrication, low-cost, fast, and specificity are the additional advantages of the sensor. The sensor was verified by the determination of Cu2+ in certified water samples, and the recovery was 97 ± 3%. In addition, the recovery of AA was 101 ± 4% by the determination of AA in commercial VC tablet. The sensor has good reproducibility and stability.

In summary, we develop a fluorescence-quenching sensor based on AuNPs to detect Cu2+ and AA, which uses the effective FRET of organic fluorescent dye and AuNPs through CuAAC. In the presence of AA, Cu2+ can be reduced to Cu+, which in turn induces CuAAC. The azide group on fluorescent dye reacts with the alkyne group on the surface of AuNPs, causing the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent dye. Through the calculation, the distance between the rhodamine fluorophore and the surface of AuNPs after conjugation is about 3.8 nm, meeting the requirement of the FRET effect. The sensor shows excellent efficiency of CuAAC and the superb quenching ability of AuNPs, which has the characteristics of simple, fast and specificity. Moreover, the sensor can be easily extended to detect Cu2+ and AA in actual samples. It doesn't need expensive instruments and complicated processing, which will have a good application prospect to be used for field testing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by National Key Research and Development Plan (2017YFE0125700) and Jiangxi Water Science and Technology Fund (202124ZDKT19).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109726.
